elements.sent-2411-001: A probabilistic approach to the stable matching problem has been identified as an important research area with several important open problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: When considering random matchings, ex-post stability is a fundamental stability concept.
elements.sent-2411-003: A prominent open problem is characterizing ex-post stability and establishing its computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-004: We investigate the computational complexity of testing ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our central result is that when either side has ties in the preferences/priorities, testing ex-post stability is NP-complete.
elements.sent-2411-006: The result even holds if both sides have dichotomous preferences.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also consider stronger versions of ex-post stability (in particular robust ex-post stability and ex-post strong stability) and prove that they can be tested in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-008: The standard mechanism used in practice is to break the ties by lottery, and then run the deferred-acceptance (DA) algorithm of Gale and Shapley for finding a stable matching \cite{GaleShapley1962 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This design was adopted in New York \cite{Atila_etal2005NY and many cities across the US.}
elements.sent-2411-010: This standard approach was criticized by Erdil and Ergin \cite{ErdilErgin2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: They demonstrated by simulations on real datasets of the New York high school admissions that, if only the course priorities have to be obeyed, but not the priorities by lottery, then the standard solution could be Pareto-improved, giving strictly better schools for about 2-3\% of the students without hurting anyone.
elements.sent-2411-012: Their proposal was however rejected by the decision makers, partly because any mechanism that improves upon the standard DA solution with lottery is inevitably manipulable \cite{Atila_etal2009NY .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Nevertheless, the intensive research on possible welfare improving mechanisms has continued.
elements.sent-2411-014: One important proposal was by Kesten \cite{Kesten2010 , who proposed the efficiency-adjusted deferred acceptance mechanism (EADAM), or in other words, school choice with consent, by relaxing the stability requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-015: If there are no priorities at all, as in the redesigned Amsterdam high school choice program \cite{RuijsOosterbeek2019 , then the DA with single tie-breaking is in fact equivalent to the classical random priority mechanism (that is also called random serial dictatorship in the literature).}
elements.sent-2411-016: In each of these mechanisms with lotteries what we end up with is a probabilistic assignment, where every student has certain (ex-ante) probabilities of getting admission to the schools in her preference list.
elements.sent-2411-017: Pareto-improvement with stochastic dominance also means that the expected welfare of each student will also increase under any cardinal utilities, hence also the total (social) welfare is increasing.
elements.sent-2411-018: Bogomolnaia and Moulin \cite{BogomolnaiaMoulin2001 showed that indeed the standard random priority solution can be sometimes Pareto-improved, and they proposed a new mechanism, the so-called probabilistic serial mechanism that always provides an ex-ante Pareto-efficient solution (with some additional desirable properties, such as envy-freeness).}
elements.sent-2411-019: The only serious drawback of this alternative mechanism is its manipulability for some cardinal utilities.
elements.sent-2411-020: Is it possible to decompose this random allocation into a convex combination of (ex-post) weakly stable matchings regarding the course priorities? We will demonstrate with Example 1 that such a case may indeed occur in practice.
elements.sent-2411-021: The resident allocation program in Israel used a simple random priority mechanism, but then they implemented two modifications: first they try to Pareto-improve the probabilities of the probabilistic assignment, and then they use a smart lottery by decomposing the probabilistic assignment into matchings where the couples are allocated to the same or geographically close hospitals \cite{Bronfman_etal2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Regarding school choice mechanism, \citet{AshlagiShi2014 proposed to improve community cohesion in school choice by a smart lottery, where the students from the same neighbourhood are matched to the same schools with high chances in the final matchings.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Such solutions can decrease the busing costs as well, which has been a crucial objective for the city of Boston, where the redesign was proposed.
elements.sent-2411-024: The novelty of our result is that in this paper the desirable matchings in the decomposition are the (weakly) stable matchings with respect to the course priorities, similarly to the proposal of Erdil and Ergin \cite{ErdilErgin2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: In other words, our method can be used to seek Pareto-improvement in school choice assignment by keeping the final solution (weakly) stable with respect to the course priorities, but it works through the improvement of the probabilistic assignment.
elements.sent-2411-026: Note that our approach leaves it open what mechanism is used to compute the initial probabilities, probabilistic serial is one option, Pareto-improvement upon the standard DA with lotteries (used also in the resident allocation in Israel) in another one.
elements.sent-2411-027: The question we are focusing on is the decomposition of a probabilistic assignment into (weakly) stable matchings.
elements.sent-2411-028: We will show that this computational question is unfortunately NP-hard, even for dichotomous preferences.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, we give an IP-based method that can compute such a decomposition, if one exists, or otherwise a decomposition, where the probability of selecting a non-stable matching is minimized.
elements.sent-2411-030: In refugee resettlement problems the refugee families have preferences over the locations, and the locations may have priorities over the refugee families \cite{DKT2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Here the complexity of the application is coming from the family sizes and their different needs in terms of services.
elements.sent-2411-032: Another standard object allocation application is course allocation, where students have preferences over bundles of courses and the courses may have weak rankings over the students, that are broken by lotteries.
elements.sent-2411-033: This is the case in the course allocation mechanism at ELTE university, the largest university in Hungary \cite{Rusznak_etal2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: A version of the probabilistic serial mechanism was proposed and implemented at TU Munich for course allocation (without priorities), where a smart lottery is conducted \cite{BichlerMerting2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The challenge in the latter decomposition is that here a feasible allocation is a many-to-many matching, where students receive bundles, and a decomposition of a probabilistic assignment into feasible allocations is only guaranteed to exist approximately, i.e., with small violations of quotas.\footnote{Finally, a simple practical example for classical one-to-one allocation problem under course, in fact dichotomous priorities, is the allocation of essay topics in the Market Design course at Corvinus University of Budapest.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Here the students can submit their preferences over the possible essay topics, and they can also claim priorities for such topics that are related to their home countries (e.g., a French student can claim priority for the topics of French teacher allocations or French university admissions).
elements.sent-2411-037: Here, the standard method used is also the DA with lottery.
elements.sent-2411-038: However, as we can see also in Example 1, the students could potentially all benefit in expectation from an alternative lottery with improved probabilistic assignment decomposed into ex-post stable matchings.
elements.sent-2411-039: For deterministic matchings, the most widely applied fairness concept is (weak) stability (also referred to as pairwise stability), which requires that there should be \new{no agent $i$ and item $o$ such that both the agent prefers the object to her existing match and either the item is unassigned or it is assigned to an agent with lower priority than $i$}\footnote{\new{The notion of weak stability of a matching is also called in the literature of school choice as justified envy freeness (or simply fairness) for allocated objects and non-wastefulness for unallocated ones.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: A stronger version of the concept (referred to as strong stability) requires that in a weakly blocking pair either the preference of the agent or her priority for the item can be weak.
elements.sent-2411-041: Both concepts are well-understood in the context of deterministic matchings but less so in the context of probabilistic matchings.
elements.sent-2411-042: Whereas much of the matching literature focuses on deterministic matchings, there is increased focus on probabilistic matchings.
elements.sent-2411-043: There are several reasons randomization is useful including allowing a richer outcome space (may be essential to achieve fairness properties such as anonymity and (ex-ante) equal-treatment-of-equals, or to satisfy certain distributional constraints in expectation) and also to capture time sharing arrangements~\citep{Aziz18a,RRV93a,TeSe98a,DoYi16a .}
elements.sent-2411-044: A random matching specifies the probability with which each agent gets a particular item.
elements.sent-2411-045: In graph-theoretical terms, it is a fractional matching.
elements.sent-2411-046: When there is a need to both consider ex-ante probabilistic constraints as well as the requirement to achieve stability ex-post, a fundamental algorithmic problem that arises is the following one:.
elements.sent-2411-047: Can a given random matching be represented as a probability distribution over (weakly) stable integral / deterministic matchings?.
elements.sent-2411-048: The problem above also captures the problem of checking whether a given random matching is ex-post stable or not.\footnote{The complexity of the problem was highlighted as an open problem by Jay Sethuraman at the Dagstuhl Workshop on Matching Under Preferences: Theory and Practice~\citep{ABF+21a .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: This problem will be the central focus of our paper.
elements.sent-2411-050: The problem is intrinsically linked to the open problem highlighted by \citet{KeUn15a concerning the characterization of ex-post stability when matchings are allowed to be random.}
elements.sent-2411-051: We show that when either agents have ties in their preferences or items have ties in their priorities, testing ex-post stability is NP-complete.
elements.sent-2411-052: In particular, we prove two complexity results: (1) deciding whether a random matching $p$ is ex-post stable is NP-complete, even if one side has strict and the other has dichotomous preferences and (2) deciding whether a random matching $p$ is ex-post stable is NP-complete, even if both sides have dichotomous preferences.
elements.sent-2411-053: The results also give an explanation on why a simple and computationally tractable characterization of ex-post stability has eluded researchers.
elements.sent-2411-054: If one proves theorems about properties and the behavior of NP-complete problems, then this usually involves lots of tedious case analysis.'' \new{To handle this NP-hard problem, we provide an integer programming formulation, that allows us to find a decomposition of a random matching $p$ with a maximum probability of being weakly stable.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We then turn our attention to stronger versions of ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-056: We show that there is a polynomial time algorithm for testing ex-post strong stability and also find a decomposition into a convex combination of integral strongly stable matchings (if it exists).
elements.sent-2411-057: We also prove a similar result for robust ex-post stability that is another stability concept.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our complexity results are summarized in Table~\ref{table:summary .}
elements.figure-2411-001: 0.75{ \begin{tabular}{lllll} ex-post ex-post strong robust ex-post stability stability stability Restriction strict prefs, strict priorities in P \citep{TeSe98a} in P (Th \ref{th:strongfrac}) in P (Th \ref{th:robust}) dichotomous prefs, dichotomous priorities NP-complete (Th \ref{th:bothdich}) in P (Th \ref{th:strongfrac}) in P (Th \ref{th:robust}) strict prefs, dichotomous priorities NP-complete (Th \ref{ex-post-verif:strict-dich}) iin P (Th \ref{th:strongfrac}) in P (Th \ref{th:robust}) dichotomous prefs, strict priorities NP-complete (Th \ref{ex-post-verif:strict-dich}) in P (Th \ref{th:strongfrac}) in P (Th \ref{th:robust}) no restrictions NP-complete (Th \ref{th:bothdich}) in P (Th \ref{th:strongfrac}) in P (Th \ref{th:robust}) \end{tabular} } \caption{Complexity of testing stability} \label{table:summary}
elements.sent-2411-059: The theory of stable matchings has a long history with several books written on the topic~\citep{GuIr89a,Manl13a,RoSo90a .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In the theory of matching under preferences, \citet{RRV93a presented several results regarding the stable matching polytope (when there are no ties) that also provide insights into random stable matching.}
elements.sent-2411-061: While the stable marriage problem was originally introduced only for strict preferences \cite{GaSh62a , for which a stable matching always exists and a simple and elegant algorithm can always find it, it turned out later that ties tend to have quite a big impact on the complexity of several related problems.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The first paper to introduce ties was the paper of Irving \cite{Irving , who gave efficient algorithms to find weakly, strongly and super stable matchings respectively, which correspond to the three natural generalization of stability depending one whether two, one, or zero agents must strictly improve in a blocking pair.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Manlove et al. \cite{manlovehard showed that several problems related to stable marriage becomes NP-hard if ties are included, the most famous one being the problem of finding a weakly stable matching of maximum size.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In this paper, we focus on the setting when items also have priorities.
elements.sent-2411-065: Our focus is also on stability concepts.
elements.sent-2411-066: They present a weak stability concept called claimwise stability and propose an algorithm to achieve it.
elements.sent-2411-067: We use the formal model presented by \citet{AzKl19b .}
elements.sent-2411-068: We consider the classic matching setting in which there are $n$ agents and $n$ items.
elements.sent-2411-069: The agents have preferences over items and items have priorities over agents.
elements.sent-2411-070: The preference relation of an agent $i\in N$ over items is denoted by $\pref_i$ where $\succ_i$ denotes the strict part of the preference and $\sim_i$ denotes the indifference part.
elements.sent-2411-071: The priority relation of an item $o\in O$ over agents is denoted by $\pref_o$ where $\succ_o$ denotes the strict part of the preference and $\sim_o$ denotes the indifference part.
elements.sent-2411-072: We say that a preference or priority relation is dichotomous if the items (agents respectively) are partitioned in at most two indifference classes.
elements.sent-2411-073: We say that a preference or priority relation is strict if there is no indifference between any two items (agents respectively).
elements.sent-2411-074: A random matching $p$ is a bistochastic $n\times n$ matrix $[p(i,o)]_{i\in N,o\in O}$, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{LE1}for each pair (i,o)\in N\times O,\ p(i,o)\geq 0,\end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{LE2}for each i\in N,\ \sum_{o\in O}p(i,o)=1, and \end{equation} \begin{equation}\label{LE3}for each o\in O,\ \sum_{i\in N}p(i,o)=1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-075: Random matchings are often also referred to as fractional matchings \citep{TeSe98a .}
elements.sent-2411-076: For each pair $(i,o)\in N\times O$, the value $p(i,o)$ represents the probability of item $o$ being matched to agent $i$.
elements.sent-2411-077: A random matching $p$ is deterministic if for each pair $(i,o)\in N\times O$, $ p(i,o) \in \{0,1\}$.
elements.sent-2411-078: Alternatively, a deterministic matching is an integer solution to linear system (\ref{LE1 ), (\ref{LE2 ), and (\ref{LE3 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: By \cite{Birk46a , each random matching can be represented as a convex combination of deterministic matchings: a decomposition of a random matching $p$ into deterministic matchings $M_j$ ($j\in \{1,\ldots,k\}$) equals a sum $p=\sum_{j=1 ^k \lambda_jM_j$ such that for each $j\in \{1,\ldots,k\}$, $\lambda_j\in [0,1]$ and $\sum_{j=1 ^k\lambda_j=1$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: A deterministic matching $p$ is weakly stable if it satisfies the following inequalities: for each pair $(i,o)\in N\times O$, \begin{equation}\label{LE4W} \sum_{o':o'\succsim_i o}p(i,o')+ \sum_{j:j\succsim_{o}i}p(j,o)-p(i,o)\geq 1.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-081: If one breaks all preference and priority ties, then the well-known deferred-acceptance algorithm \citep{GaSh62a computes a deterministic matching that is weakly stable.}
elements.sent-2411-082: We now introduce the central concept of this paper, ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, while there exists mechanisms that compute (weakly) stable matchings, and there exists many mechanisms that compute lotteries that are ex-ante fair, (e.g. Pareto-optimal), most of these do not guarantee ex-post stability too.
elements.sent-2411-084: Since stability is an important property in resident allocation, school choice and similar applications, it is natural to aim for implementing the lottery in a way such that the outcome is guaranteed to be weakly stable, or has a maximum probability of being weakly stable, since blocking pairs may cause certain deviations in the future, destabilizing the market and decreasing trust in the system.
elements.sent-2411-085: Hence, determining the ex-post stability of a random matching $p$ is indeed a highly important task.
elements.sent-2411-086: While our main result shows that this problem is NP-hard even in very restricted settings, we give an algorithm utilizing integer programming techniques that not only decides this, but even if the answer is no, it finds a decomposition with a maximum probability of being weakly stable.
elements.sent-2411-087: Hence, if we have an algorithm to determine ex-post stability of a matching, then we can look for such Pareto-improvements for the agents and check whether the resulting random matching is still ex-post stable, and if yes, then this allows each agent to improve in the solution without hurting ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-088: We have four agents $a,b,c,d$ and four items $o_1,o_2,o_3,o_4$ with the following priorities.
elements.sent-2411-089: Furthermore, this matching can also be implemented as a lottery over weakly stable matchings $M_1=\{(a,o_1),(b,o_4),(c,o_3),(d,o_2)\}$ and $M_2=\{ (a,o_3),(b,o_1),(c,o_2),(d,o_4)\}$ with probability $\frac{1 {2}$ each.}
elements.section-2411-004: Ex post stability: Algorithm and Characterization Under Absence of Ties
elements.sent-2411-090: Indeed, given an arbitrary instance $I$, where the preferences can be incomplete and the number of agents and items can be different, we can create an equivalent instance $I'$ with $|O'|=|N'|$ and complete preferences as follows.
elements.sent-2411-091: Each agent's preference is extended by first appending $o_i$ being strictly worse than the originally acceptable items and then the rest of $O'$ tied, but strictly worse than $o_i$.
elements.sent-2411-092: Then, we add $|O'|-|N|$ dummy agents to $N$, who are indifferent between all items, obtaining $N'$.
elements.sent-2411-093: The priorities of the dummy items $o_i$ are $i\succ [N'\setminus i]$, where brackets indicate a single tie.
elements.sent-2411-094: Finally, the items from $O$ have their priorities extended by ranking the rest of the agents in a tie, but being strictly worse than any originally acceptable one.
elements.sent-2411-095: Furthermore, for a deterministic stable matching $M'$ of $I'$, any agent of $N$ must be with an originally acceptable item or with his dummy item, so if we delete all $(i,o)$ pairs from it that correspond to dummy items/agents, or originally unacceptable pairs, then we must get a deterministic stable matching of $I$.
elements.sent-2411-096: We next observe that when both preferences and priorities are strict, then ex-post stability admits both a simple characterization, concise geometric description and also a polynomial-time algorithm to test ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-097: Note that if ties are present, then fractional stability does not imply ex-post stability~ \citep{AzKl19b We note that ex-post stability can be tested in linear-time if preferences / priorities on both sides are strict.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Furthermore, if a given a random matching is ex-post stable, there exists a polynomial-time algorithm to represent the random matching as a lottery over deterministic stable matchings.
elements.sent-2411-099: Under strict preferences and priorities, ex-post stability and fractional stability are equivalent~\citep{AzKl19b .}
elements.sent-2411-100: So we just need to check for the linear constraints capturing fractional stability.
elements.sent-2411-101: In the strict preference setting, any fractional stable matching (equivalently ex-post stable matching) can be efficiently decomposed to a convex combination of deterministic stable matchings~\citep{TeSe98a .}

elements.sent-2411-234: First, we compute the maximum value $|\lambda |_1= \sum_{l=1 ^{n^2+1}\lambda_l$ such that there are weakly stable matchings $M_1,\dots, M_{n^2+1}$ with $\sum_{l=1 ^{n^2+1}\lambda_lM_l \le p$ (coordinate-wise) with the integer program IP \eqref{IP:start -\eqref{IP:end . \begin{equation}max \sum_{l=1}^{n^2+1} \lambda_l \label{IP:start} s.t. \sum_{o'\succsim_i o}M_l(i,o')+ \sum_{j\succsim_{o}i;}M_l(j,o) -M_l(i,o) \geq 1 ( (i,o)\in N\times O, l\in [n^2+1] ) \sum_{l=1}^{n^2+1} \lambda_l\cdot M_l(i,o) \leq p(i,o) ((i,o) \in N\times O) \sum_o M_l(i,o) =1 (i\in N) \sum_iM_l(i,o) =1 (o\in O) \lambda_l \geq 0 (l\in [n^2+1]) M_l(i,o) \in \{ 0,1\} ((i,o)\in N\times O, l\in [n^2+1])\label{IP:end}\end{equation} \new{Then in the second step, we write $\frac{1 {1-|\lambda |_1 (p-\sum_{l=1 ^{n^2+1}\lambda_lM_l)$ as a convex combination of deterministic matchings and then combine these two convex combinations into one, see Algorithm 1.}}}}}}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-001:
\label{alg:1} \caption{Decomposing a random matching with maximum probability of being weakly stable}\label{alg:cap}  egin{enumerate} \item 
ew{Compute an optimal solution $(\lambda^*,M^*)$ of IP \eqref{IP:start}-\eqref{IP:end}.} \item 
ew{Let $p'= rac{1}{1-|\lambda^*|_1}(p-\sum_{l=1}^{n^2+1}\lambda_l M^*_l)$. This again gives a bistochastic matrix.} \item 
ew{Compute a decomposition of $p'$ into $n^2$ perfect matchings using the Birkhoff-Neumann algorithm: $p'=\sum_{l=n^2+2}^{2n^2+1}\lambda_l M_i$.} \item 
ew{Return $(M^*_1,\dots,M_{n^2+1}^*,M_{n^2+2}, \dots, M_{2n^2+1})$ and $(\lambda^*_1,\dots,\lambda^*_{n^2+1},(1-|\lambda^*|_1)\cdot \lambda_{n^2+2},\dots, (1-|\lambda^*|_1)\cdot \lambda_{2n^2+1})$} \end{enumerate}

elements.sent-2411-236: Then, $\frac{1 {|\lambda'|_1}\hat{p}$ is in the convex hull of weakly stable matchings.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Therefore, by Caratheodory's theorem, we get that there are $n^2+1$ matchings $M_1'',\dots, M_{n^2+1 ''$ with $(\lambda_1'',\dots, \lambda_{n^2+1 '')$, such that $\frac{1 {|\lambda'|_1}\hat{p = \sum_{l=1 ^{n^2+1}\lambda_l''M_l''$ and $|\lambda''|_1=1$.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-239: In this section, we consider stronger versions of ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-240: Robust ex-post stability, is a natural strengthening of ex-post stability~\citep{AzKl19b .}
elements.sent-2411-241: It follows easily that if we restrict attention to deterministic matchings, then both ex-post stability and robust ex-post stability coincide with weak stability and no envy (Definition~\ref{def:weakstab ).}
elements.sent-2411-242: Checking whether a given random matching $p$ is robust ex-post stable is polynomial-time solvable.
elements.sent-2411-243: For each $i\in N$ and $o\in O$, we check whether there exists an integral matching $q$ consistent with $p$ such that $i$ is not matched to $o$ under $q$ and $(i,o)$ form a blocking pair for $q$.
elements.sent-2411-244: This can be checked by testing whether there exists an allocation in which $i$ is matched to some item $o'$ such that $o\succ_i o'$ and $p(i,o')>0$; $o$ is matched to some agent $j\in N$ such that $i\succ_o j$ and $p(j,o)>0$; and each other agent $k$ is matched to some item $o''$ such that $p(k,o'')>0$.
elements.sent-2411-245: This test can be solved in polynomial time by checking whether the underlying bipartite graph with the admissible edges admits a perfect matching.
elements.sent-2411-246: In this section, we consider a stability concept called ex-post strong stability which is based on a concept called strong stability.
elements.sent-2411-247: Equivalently, it satisfies the following two conditions.
elements.sent-2411-248: Clearly strong stability implies weak stability.
elements.sent-2411-249: Note that under strict preferences, strong stability and weak stability are equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-250: A strongly stable matching may not exist but there is a linear-time algorithm to check if it exists or not and to find one if it exists~\citep{KMMP07a .}
elements.sent-2411-251: The notion of strong stability for integral matchings lends itself to two natural stability concepts for the case of random matchings.
elements.sent-2411-252: Clearly ex-post strong stability implies ex-post stability.
elements.sent-2411-253: Fractional strong stability implies fractional stability.
elements.sent-2411-254: Suppose, the first condition of fractional strong stability is satisfied: for all $(i,o)$, $\sum_{o'\succ_i o p(i,o')+ \sum_{i'\succ_o i p(i',o)+\sum_{o'\sim_{i o p(i,o')\ge 1$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Then, for all $(i,o)$, $\sum_{o'\succsim_i o; o'\neq o p(i,o')+ \sum_{i'\succ_o i p(i',o)+ p(i,o)\ge 1$ which means that fractional stability is satisfied.}}
elements.sent-2411-256: Next, we establish an equivalence between ex-post strong stability and fractional strong stability.
elements.sent-2411-257: For weak preferences and priorities, there exists a polynomial-time algorithm to test ex-post strong stability and in case the answer is yes, there is a polynomial-time algorithm to find its representation as a convex combination of strongly stable deterministic matchings.
elements.sent-2411-258: Strong ex-post stability can be checked in polynomial time as follows.
elements.sent-2411-259: Strong ex-post stability is equivalent to fractional strong stability (Lemma~\ref{lemma:convexhull ).}
elements.sent-2411-260: Fractional strong stability can be checked by considering $2|N|\times |O| $ inequalities used in the definition of fractional strong stability.
elements.sent-2411-261: For a matching that satisfies fractional strong stability, it lies in the convex hull of the set of deterministic strongly stable matchings.
elements.sent-2411-262: Such a matching can be represented by a convex combination of strongly stable deterministic matchings by an algorithm of \citet{Kuny18a that uses a similar argument as that of \citet{TeSe98a .}}
elements.sent-2411-263: In the proof of Theorem~\ref{th:strongfrac , we invoke an algorithmic result of \citet{Kuny18a .}}
elements.sent-2411-264: For the sake of completeness and exposition, we give a description of the algorithm of \citet{Kuny18a .}

elements.sent-2411-287: We undertook a study of testing stability of random matchings.
elements.sent-2411-288: Our central results are that testing ex-post stability is NP-complete even under severe restrictions.
elements.sent-2411-289: The computational hardness result also explains why a combinatorially simple and tractable characterization has eluded mathematicians and economists.
elements.sent-2411-290: Nonetheless, we provided a way to find an optimal decomposition using integer programming.
elements.sent-2411-291: We also considered stronger versions of ex-post stability and presented polynomial-time algorithms for testing them.
elements.sent-2411-292: A natural research direction is to understand sufficient conditions on the preferences and priorities under which testing ex-post stability is polynomial-time solvable.
elements.sent-2411-293: Yet another research problem is understanding the conditions under which stability concepts coincide.
elements.sent-2411-294: Parameterized algorithms for the computationally hard problems is yet another research direction.
